basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Aʿlā — “The Most High” — bears one of the loftiest of the Divine Names. The sūra opens with a command: Glorify the Name of your Lord the Most High, and Qushayrī unfolds it from the outset in its full amplitude: “Glorify your Lord through the knowledge of His Names, and swim by your inmost secret in the oceans of His sublimity, and draw from the jewels of His loftiness and splendour wherewith to set the necklace of His praise.” Then come four divine acts in cascade — to create and harmonise, to measure and guide, to send forth the pasture, to render it to ashes — which sketch in four images the whole work of the Most High upon His creation. At the heart of the sūra, verse 3 (qaddara fa-hadā, “He measured and He guided”) gives Qushayrī occasion for one of the deepest pages of his commentary: a ladder of guidances, from the most outward to the most inward, that culminates in the silence of the unifiers: “When they rose above the limit of proof, then above the limit of clarity, then above what still resembles seeing — they knew that He is beyond reach, beyond every joining and every parting. So they returned to the abode of incapacity, and there made their pillow.” The sūra, descended according to tradition early in the Meccan preaching, closes on the timeless promise: this is already in the Books of Abraham and Moses. The way of the Most High is of always.

versets.trad: Glorify the Name of your Lord the Most High,
versets.commentaire: An opening verse that sets all that follows. sabbiḥ (سَبِّحْ): glorify, declare the absolute transcendence — this is the tasbīḥ, the act by which the soul clears God of all imperfection and every likeness. And one does not glorify God in general — one glorifies His Name. Qushayrī opens the verse in three concentric movements: “Glorify your Lord through the knowledge of His Names (asmāʾihi, أَسْمَائِه); and swim, by your inmost secret (sirr, سِرّ), in the oceans of His sublimity (ʿalāʾihi, عَلَائِه); and draw from the jewels of His loftiness and splendour wherewith to set the necklace of His praise (madḥihi wa-thanāʾihi, مَدْحِه وَثَنَائِه).” Knowledge, inward immersion, the weaving of praise — three stages of one glorification. al-Aʿlā, “the Most High,” is one of the most essential Names: the One who is above every height, whom no ascent reaches.

mots.def: sabbaḥa: to declare God pure of every defect, to glorify; same root as tasbīḥ.
ism: the Name; in the construct accusative.
aʿlā: superlative of ʿālī; the Most High. Divine Name.

versets.commentaire: The first divine act. khalaqa fa-sawwā: He created, then He harmonised. Qushayrī, soberly: “He created every being endowed with spirit, then He adjusted its parts and composed its members according to the wondrous order and exquisite composition He has reserved for it.” To create is not enough: one must balance. Taswiya — already encountered in sūra 91:7 — is that divine finishing that makes each creature itself, in accord with its own end.

mots.def: sawwā: intensive form; to harmonise, to adjust, to set in balance.

versets.commentaire: Central verse. qaddara fa-hadā: He measured, then He guided. Qushayrī begins with the immediate sense — “He determined what He created according to His measure; and He guided every living being toward what benefits it, inspiring it to take what nourishes it and to leave what harms it — by way of direct inspiration (ilhām, إِلْهَام).” Then, in a few prodigious lines, he unfolds a ladder of guidances according to hearts: “He guided the hearts of the heedless toward the seeking of this world, and they peopled it. He guided the hearts of the worshippers toward the seeking of the hereafter, and they preferred it. He guided the hearts of the ascetics toward the impermanence of the world, and they cast it away. He guided the hearts of the learned toward the contemplation of His signs and the arguing from His works, and they came to know those signs and clung to them. He guided the hearts of the disciples (murīdūn, مُرِيدُون) toward the glory of His attribute, and they preferred it, and they spent their utmost effort to seek it. He guided the hearts of the gnostics (ʿārifūn, عَارِفُون) toward the holiness of His nature: they watched for Him, then contemplated Him. He guided the hearts of the unifiers (muwaḥḥidūn, مُوَحِّدُون) toward the sublimity of His kingship in the singleness of His grandeur, and they left all that is other than He, and they parted from every familiar — until they aimed at Him alone.” And the summit: “When they rose above the limit of proof, then above the limit of clarity, then above what still resembles seeing — they knew that He is beyond reach, and that He is beyond every joining and parting (faṣl wa-waṣl, فَصْل وَوَصْل). So they returned to the abode of incapacity (mawṭin al-ʿajz, مَوْطِن العَجْز), and there made their pillow.” The ladder closes on ʿajz — the confession of incapacity as the crown of knowledge. The more one knows God, the more one knows that one does not know Him.

mots.def: qaddara: to determine, to measure, to decree; same root as qadar.
hadā: to guide, to lead in the way.

versets.commentaire: akhraja al-marʿā: He brought forth the pasture. Qushayrī, in one word: “the vegetation.” The verse honours plant life, which is often neglected in inventories of blessings. But without the pasture, no beast — and thus no human — would feed. Before every living thing, the green grass.

mots.def: akhraja: to bring out, to make grow.
marʿā: place of grazing, vegetation; noun of place from raʿā (to graze).

versets.trad: then turns it into dark, withered stubble!
versets.commentaire: ghuthāʾ (غُثَاء) — Qushayrī: “He made it withered, like the debris carried by the flood.” aḥwā (أَحْوَى): dark brown, almost black. A rough and exact image: the vegetation that shone in spring, by the dry season, is swept away like a dried wick. All that turns green is promised to turn brown. The sūra does not linger on this sadness — it states it simply, as a fact of the decree. And it is in this shadow that the next image (the Quran which will not be lost) takes on all its light.

mots.def: jaʿala: to make, to turn into; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ghuthāʾ: what the torrent carries (debris, scum), withered vegetation.

versets.trad: We shall make you recite [the Quran], and you will not forget,
versets.commentaire: An intimate promise to the Prophet. sa-nuqriʾuka: We shall make you recite. Qushayrī: “We shall gather the Quran in your heart — O Muḥammad — by memory, until you do not forget, for We Ourselves preserve it for you.” The preservation of the Quran is not only outward (the writing, the transmission): it is inward, guaranteed in the very heart of the Prophet. Junayd adds, finely: “You will not forget the practice that flows from it” — that is: you will not lose only the words, you will not lose the just gesture they call for either.

mots.def: aqraʾa: to make read or recite; in the imperfect 1st pers. plur. + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

versets.ar: إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ
versets.tr: illā mā shāʾa Llāhu innahu yaʿlamu l-jahra wa-mā yakhfā
versets.trad: except what God wills [you to forget]. Truly, He knows the manifest and what is hidden.
versets.commentaire: A delicate verse. Qushayrī specifies: “save what does not fall under the prescription, and what you might forget before transmission, with no obligation upon you to transmit it.” All that had to pass from heaven to the world has been retained and transmitted; but God Himself keeps the final detail of forgetting and remembering. And the end of the verse recalls, as a signature: He knows the manifest (al-jahr) and what is hidden (mā yakhfā). The two dimensions, outer and inner, belong to a single knowing.

mots.def: jahr: what is said or shown openly.

versets.trad: We shall ease your way to ease.
versets.commentaire: A personal promise to the Prophet, but whose tone covers every soul that entrusts itself to God. nuyassiruka li-l-yusrā: We shall make you easy to the easy way. The same formula as in sūra 92:7. Where the servant aligns, God lightens; and the lightening is not a dispensation, it is a grace that makes what is right desirable.

mots.def: yassara: to make easy; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

versets.trad: So give the Reminder, for the Reminder is profitable.
versets.commentaire: fa-dhakkir: so remind. Qushayrī makes a delicate remark on the particle in (“if”): “The reminder profits, beyond doubt. But it profits the one whom God has granted the favour of letting himself be taught by it. As for the one whose denial is known, he is as in the verse: ‘What benefit has the brother of this world from his eyes, when before him light and darkness become equal?'” The reminder asks one to still have the capacity to discern; for the one who has blinded himself, even the clearest verses look like darkness.

mots.def: nafaʿa: to be useful, to profit.

versets.trad: The one who fears [God] shall heed it,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “the one who fears God and fears His punishment.” khashya (خَشْية), the reverential fear of which Tustarī said, at sūra 98, that it is secret (sirr) before being visible. It is that fear that makes the ear permeable to the reminder. The reminder does not create the fear; it comes to nourish a fear that was already present, even if only in seed.

versets.trad: while the wretched will turn away from it.
versets.commentaire: yatajannabuhā: he avoids it. The next verse will name what the ashqā avoids: not God, but the reminder. It is the reminder he flees — as one turns away from a mirror in which one does not want to see oneself.

mots.def: tajannaba: to avoid, to keep away from; with feminine suffix pronoun (referring to dhikrā).
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ashqā: superlative of shaqī; the most wretched.

versets.trad: He shall burn in the vast Fire
versets.commentaire: al-nār al-kubrā: the great Fire. Its greatness is the terrible counterweight to al-aʿlā of verse 1: He the Most High, this the most great. The one who did not look upward finds himself before the immense below.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ
versets.tr: thumma lā yamūtu fīhā wa-lā yaḥyā
versets.trad: where, then, he will neither die nor live.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, in few words: “he does not die a death that gives him relief, and he does not live a life that delights him.” There is the worst of the Fire: not so much suffering, as the absence of an exit. Neither toward the rest of death, nor toward the joy of life. A suspension without term.

versets.trad: Blessed is the one who purifies himself,
versets.commentaire: A luminous verse. aflaḥa: blessed, prospered; tazakkā: he has purified himself, has made himself grow. Qushayrī gathers in one phrase the whole teaching: “The one who has purified himself of sins and defects, and of contemplating creatures, and who has paid the zakāt — that one has found salvation, attained his goal, and won what he sought.” Note the order: (1) outer sins; (2) inner defects; (3) contemplation of creatures (mushāhadat al-khalq) — no longer measuring one's existence by the gaze of others; (4) effective almsgiving. Four successive purifications, which are also four thresholds.

mots.def: tazakkā: to purify oneself, to sanctify; same root as zakāt.

versets.trad: who invokes the Name of his Lord and performs the prayer.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives two readings: (1) “he invoked the Name of his Lord in his prayer” — that is, in the liturgical tasbīḥ and takbīr; (2) “he invoked Him by unicity (al-waḥdāniyya, الوَحْدَانِيَّة), and prayed for Him.” Prayer is not only the rite; it is also the disposition that precedes it: declaring God One, and praying for Him — not for oneself. The verse thus loops the sūra back to its opening: one glorifies the Name, one invokes the Name, one entrusts oneself to the Name.

mots.def: ṣallā: to pray; intensive form of the ritual prayer.

versets.trad: Yet you prefer the life of this world
versets.commentaire: tuʾthirūna: you prefer — from the verb āthara, to choose one in setting aside the other. Qushayrī: “you incline toward it, and place your share of it above the rights of God, exalted is He.” Preference is not in the abstract choice: it is in the weight the soul gives to things at the moment of acting.

mots.def: dunyā: the (life) closest, this world; literally “the lowest.”

versets.trad: while the life to come is better and more lasting.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives the sense in full: “the hereafter, for the believers, is better and more lasting — than this present life is for its seekers.” Two adjectives accumulated: better (in quality) and more lasting (in length of time). The whole balance of the spiritual life is summed up in this inner weighing: what passes weighs less, in the end, than what endures.

mots.def: abqā: superlative of bāqī; the more lasting, that which remains.

versets.trad: Truly, this is in the ancient Scriptures:
versets.commentaire: al-ṣuḥuf al-ūlā: the ancient pages — the Quranic name for the Scriptures revealed before the Quran. Qushayrī, in a few words: “this admonition is in the Scriptures that preceded; and likewise in the pages of Abraham, Moses, and others. For tawḥīd, the promise and the warning, do not differ according to the variations of the revealed Laws.” Tawḥīd is constant across the ages; what changes is the Law (sharīʿa) that formulates it for its time.

mots.def: ṣuḥuf: plural of ṣaḥīfa; pages, Scriptures revealed.

versets.trad: the Books of Abraham and Moses.
versets.commentaire: The sūra closes with two names that are also two figures of universal Islam: Ibrāhīm, the friend of God (khalīl Allāh), to whom the original prayer was revealed; Mūsā, God's interlocutor (kalīm Allāh), to whom the Law was given. That the message of this sūra — glorify the Name, be saved by purification — is already found in their pages says that the spiritual way is of always. The novelty of the Quran is not a break; it is a faithfulness resumed.

mots.def: ṣuḥuf: pages, Scriptures; in the construct genitive.
Ibrāhīm: proper name of the prophet Abraham.
Mūsā: proper name of the prophet Moses.
